div.head: HA–4. Letter from the Ambassador in Haiti (Drew) to the Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs (Rubottom)Source: Department of State, Rubottom Files, Lot 60 D 553, “Haiti 1958.” Secret; Official-Informal.
div.p: I am beginning to have increasingly serious qualms about the establishment of a Marine Corps Military Mission to Haiti.On April 1, 1958, Ambassador Drew had reported that during a conversation on March 31, President François Duvalier had stated repeatedly and categorically that Haiti wanted a United States military mission composed of Marine Crops officers. Duvalier explained that it was the Marines “who founded and trained the Haitian Army and [who] understand us.” (Telegram 416 from Port-au-Prince; 738.58/4–158) Subsequently, the Haitian government sent a military mission to the United States with instructions to request the United States Government to dispatch a military mission to Haiti to reorganize the Haitian Army. As a result of these discussions, the Department of Defense, with concurrence of the Department of State, authorized the Department of the Navy to discuss with the Haiti government an agreement to augment the U.S. Naval Mission, currently established in Haiti, so as to accommodate a U.S. Marine Corps Mission. (Letter from Assistant Secretary of Defense for International Security Affairs Mansfield D. Sprague to Rubottom, April 29, 1958; 738.58/4–2958)

Until quite recently it could honestly be said that a semblance of democracy existed in Haiti. In the past five or six weeks, however, things have changed and I would now describe the existing regime as a “Police State” clothed in the trappings of democracy.
A convenient cut-off date for this drastic change can be taken as May 2 when martial law and a brief curfew were established.

Prior to that date there was a semblance of an independent or opposition press composed of Haiti Miroir and Independance. The editors and some of the staff of these papers are now in jail and their plants were wrecked by unidentified civilian bands, obviously with the approval of the Military, as the incidents occurred during a period of stringent curfew when peaceable citizens were being shot down in cold blood for violating the curfew.

The two defeated candidates for the Presidency, Louis DEJOIE and Clement JUMELLE previously enjoyed an attenuated liberty. Now, however, on the flimsy pretext of involvement in the Mahotière bomb affair, Dejoie was hounded into exile with a price on his head and Jumelle and his brother Ducasse have been similarly forced into hiding as outlaws. The pretense that either of these men were actually involved in the manufacture of bombs is not taken seriously by anyone.

After Dejoie was exiled from the country, there was some thought that Jumelle might be tolerated. His entire family was arrested recently but has been released, with the exception of a brother who is still in jail.
The Colombian Minister informed me that the Assistant Manager of the Haitian-Colombian Bank, a known Jumellist, was savagely beaten in his home by a masked gang in an effort to force him to reveal the whereabouts of Jumelle. At last account he was recovering in a hospital from the beating which left him close to death.

I would emphasize here that I am persuaded that neither Dejoie nor Jumelle have seriously conspired against the Government since the inauguration of President Duvalier, although it is probably true that some of their followers, particularly of Dejoie, may have engaged in conspiratorial plotting. A large number of political prisoners languish in jail with estimates ranging from 80 to 800 and the actual figure probably being somewhere in between.

The Army, which under General Kebreau was at least a counterpoise to the civilian element of the Government, now appears to be under the complete domination of Dr. Duvalier. It is difficult to believe anything else when we have a photograph in our possession of the head of the Secret Police, Clement BARBOT, striding behind the President with a sub-machine gun under his arm, his military escort bringing up the rear, during the Flag Day celebration at Arcaihaie on May 18. To the best of my knowledge the Secret Police continues to operate with the tolerance, if not the support, of the Army.

The financial assistance to the Duvalier Administration which has thus far been forthcoming, or is in prospect, from United States and international sources will help to perpetuate the present regime. Looking at it for what it basically is, namely, aid to the people of Haiti, I find it entirely defensible and would even go so far as to support additional amounts of aid in the future.

However, I think we should do some careful soul-searching before we agree to send a Military Mission to Haiti, particularly one of such composition and size that it will practically guarantee the continuance in office of Dr. Duvalier to the end of his six-year term in 1963. Of course, we want stability in Haiti but, particularly having in mind the critical examination of our policies in Latin America provoked by the recent incidents involving the Vice President, should we agree to the establishment if the Mission without establishing any conditions precedent?

I find myself becoming increasingly repelled by the thought of sending a Mission in here when the jails are crammed with political prisoners, many presumably innocent and none of whom have been given the benefit of a fair trial; when defeated candidates and their supporters, many of whom have asked for nothing but peace and harmony, are beaten, tortured and hounded into exile; when a restrained opposition press has been ruthlessly snuffed out of existence; and when masked night riders, reminiscent of the Ku Klux Klan, operate from their headquarters in the National Palace. Aside from the question of our own conscience, I think we should give particular thought to the repercussions
in the United States and abroad if we go through with the establishment of the Mission without some effort to correct the conditions I describe.

I would like to suggest that we consider conveying to President Duvalier the thought that the abolishment of the Secret Police, the release or trial of political prisoners, the restoration of the two suppressed newspapers to the status quo ante, and a halt to hounding of political opposition are prior conditions to our agreement to a Military Mission. There may be other conditions we may wish to include, but these are the main ones which occur to me now.

If my recommendation is adopted, we will have to consider how to approach the President without upsetting our present friendly relations or arousing cries of “intervention”. Obviously it will be a difficult and delicate assignment.

In closing, I wish to make it quite clear that I am heartily in favor of establishing a Military Mission here, preferably composed of members of the United States Marine Corps as requested by the Haitian Government. I can think of no surer guarantee of stability for the foreseeable future. However, I repeat, we should consider carefully whether we can justify the establishment of such a Mission to a Government responsible for the measures which I describe.
